# **CHAPTER 1**

###  **INTRODUCTION**

Agriculture​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ continues to be a very vital sector of the economy in the Philippines, and it employs almost one-fifth of the total workforce of the country. Moreover, it is the main source of income for rural areas and also provides food security. Nevertheless, only a few smallholder and family farmers have the means to practice modern farming; thus, they still depend on traditional irrigation and fertilization methods that require a lot of manual labor and are susceptible to climate changes. As the extreme weather events become more frequent in different parts of the Philippines, such traditional farming methods are becoming more and more vulnerable, which eventually leads to the decrease of both productivity and resilience (World Bank, 2019). In such a situation, the use of renewable energy—mainly solar power—with sensors and Internet of Things (IoT) devices to modernize farming is a very viable solution. The country, the Philippines, is very compatible with such a change: among other things, it has a very strong solar potential, which is estimated to be around 4.5–5.5 kWh/m² per day, and the government’s support towards smart and green energy solutions is increasing (Department of Energy \[DOE\], 2020). The installation of solar-powered IoT devices in multi-crop row household gardens can facilitate the efficient use of water and nutrients, labor can be saved, and productivity can be increased in resource-constrained environments (World Bank, 2019; DOE, 2020).

Innovations in IoT, automation, and the use of renewables in agriculture have attracted a lot of research interest in a short time. The previous researches have shown that IoT application in precision farming leads to improvement of the environmental sustainability, increased yields, and better resource efficiency (Shamshiri et al., 2018; Navarro et al., 2020). For instance, the sensors built into the soil can gauge the moisture, temperature, pH, or electrical conductivity, and the internet-connected devices can start the pumps or valves, thus enabling real-time irrigation control. Navarro and his colleagues (2020), after thoroughly reviewing the farm IoT implementations, came to a conclusion that such systems provide opportunities for the ongoing soil moisture and environmental parameters monitoring, which in turn results in saving water and increasing crop output. The narrative reviews in the Philippines reveal that there is a growing trend in the use of IoT devices for crop monitoring, irrigation automation, and intelligent farming; however, there are still problems in terms of connectivity, cost, and technical capacity of rural farmers (Mastul et al., 2023). In the area of small-scale vegetable production, the national research agencies are putting the planting calendar and crop rotation as the two most important factors that contribute to the yield maximization in household gardens (Philippine Council for Agriculture, Aquatic and Natural Resources Research and Development \[PCAARRD\], n.d.).

Still, under the current systems, there are significant challenges that prevent the realization of the full potential of these promising developments. Most​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ of the agricultural IoT and solar-powered projects, which are transparently shown, are primarily intended for monoculture environments or large-scale commercial farms. Hardly any of them take into account the changing seasonal needs, crop rotation, or nutrient changes in mixed vegetable ​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌plots. Department of Agriculture says that in the Philippines, technology access in the countryside is mainly limited by the high cost and poor infrastructure, thus smallholders have a low rate of technology adoption due to these constraints (Department of Agriculture \[DA\], 2021). Also, even though a good number of irrigation systems are automated, nutrient regulation that is integrated, for example, automatic dosing of fertilizers based on soil moisture, electrical conductivity, and pH, is at a very early stage of development.According​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ to a recent study, the majority of Internet of Things (IoT) configurations are intended for greenhouses or single-crop systems, and the authors do not take into account the fluctuations of multi-crop, rotational farming in smallholder farms (Mastul et., 2023; Shamshiri et., ​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌2018). These deficiencies pinpoint the necessity for a flexible, affordable, and robust system that caters to smallholders in the Philippines.

This paper presents a proposal for an automated, solar-powered IoT-based irrigation and nutrient regulation system for urban household farming using Arduino-based sensors. The system integrates soil moisture and nutrient-related sensors with a solar energy supply to enable real-time monitoring and automated control of irrigation and nutrient delivery. When sensor readings indicate insufficient moisture or nutrient levels, pumps, valves, and dosing modules are automatically activated to supply the required water and liquid nutrients. In addition, the IoT-enabled architecture allows remote monitoring and control through a web-based or mobile interface. System performance will be evaluated based on irrigation efficiency, nutrient regulation accuracy, water-use efficiency, energy sustainability, plant growth response, and reduction in manual labor during household garden trials conducted in both dry and wet seasons. The study aims to demonstrate an affordable, data-driven, and environmentally sustainable model suitable for small-scale urban farming applications in the Philippines. 

The proposed research offers a sustainable, economical, and easily adoptable solution for urban households and community-based growers in the Philippines. The exclusive use of photovoltaic energy significantly reduces electricity costs and carbon emissions while ensuring continuous system operation. By optimizing water and nutrient delivery, the system minimizes resource waste and ensures that plants receive appropriate care according to their growth requirements. Automation further reduces the need for manual labor, making the system suitable for users with limited time or agricultural experience. Through continuous data collection and automated adjustments, the system promotes data-driven urban farming practices that can improve plant health and productivity. The technology supports national agricultural modernization initiatives, enhances household food security, and contributes to the achievement of the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals by encouraging sustainable urban and peri-urban farming goals by facilitating small-scale, community, urban, and peri-urban farming initiatives.

This research focuses on the design, development, and performance evaluation of a solar-powered IoT-based irrigation and nutrient regulation system for small-scale urban farming environments. The system emphasizes automated soil moisture monitoring, nutrient control, and operation powered solely by renewable energy. The study is limited to controlled household or small-garden settings and does not evaluate system performance in large-scale commercial agricultural operations. Factors such as long-term maintenance requirements, extreme weather conditions, and integration with advanced agricultural technologies are beyond the scope of this research. Additionally, the study does not include comprehensive economic analyses or long-term yield comparisons across different geographic regions.
