# **CHAPTER 2**

**Review of Related Literature**

**Introduction**

The​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ chapter contains the Related Readings, Literature, and Studies of both local and foreign sources that support the development of the automated solar-powered IoT-based irrigation and nutrient regulation system. These discussions extend the base of the study and show its importance in the light of the present-day agricultural practices. Also, the chapter comprises the synthesis and the rationale that serve as an aid in getting the significance of the ​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌research.

## **Related Readings**

It​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ is clear that the Philippine government wants the adoption of climate-smart and precision irrigation technologies to be the major driver in the modernization of agriculture, to enhance productivity, and to build up the sector's resilience against climate change. The policies under the Philippine Development Plan (2023–2028) are designed to promote the investments that would lead to the development of sensor-based and solar-powered irrigation systems together with automated nutrient management in order to facilitate small-scale and household agricultural practices. One​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ of the primary goals of the Department of Agriculture is to implement water-saving measures in irrigation for crop production optimization and diversification of high-value crop production through the aid of technology which is clearly needed (NEDA, 2023; DA, 2024). The Department of Agriculture is very much on board in advocating for the implementation of water-saving measures in irrigation in order to both optimize crop production and to facilitate the diversification of high-value crop production through the aid of technology which clearly shows how directly needed technologies like IoT-enabled solar irrigation systems are to achieve these national objectives (NEDA, 2023; DA, 2024).

The environment in terms of law and regulations are equally supportive and set standards for water and nutrient use in agriculture without causing any harm to the environment. RA 9275 (Philippine Clean Water Act of 2004\) defines the use of treated wastewater for irrigation as a regulated activity for which the guidelines for such use shall be established by the Department of Agriculture through administrative orders that not only certify the safety but also the technical standards for water reuse in agriculture. These laws serve as a secure legal basis for the employment of fully automated irrigation systems that can also integrate nutrient monitoring and water efficiency technologies thus, this study comes in line with the national goals to conserve water resources, advance agriculture with the help of technology and build resilience to climate change (DA-BSWM, 2007). ​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌

## **RELATED LITERATURE (Foreign and Local)**

**Urban Vertical Farming**

Urban farming literature has been consistent in pointing out the necessity of integrating smart technologies to overcome the limitations of space, resource inefficiency, and reliance on manual labor in urban and home agricultural settings. The reviews present that IoT based setups having sensor capabilities allow not only the detection of soil moisture and nutrient conditions but also the observation of environmental factors in a very accurate and timely manner. This facilitates better irrigation and nutrient management than traditional methods can offer. Among other things, the use of renewable energies, mainly solar power, is focused on as one of the major factors to take urban farming to a higher level of sustainability, not only cost reduction but also long term availability of power in remote areas. In general, the authors of the papers advocate the use of automated, sensor- based, and energy saving technologies as a good way to make urban farming sustainable and resilient, thus providing food security and green production (Zhang et al., 2022; Kumar et al., 2023).

Further empirical studies support these results. Arduino, based, IoT, enabled irrigation and nutrient regulation systems were found to drastically reduce the waste of water and fertilizer while still maintaining or even improving the growth of crops in small, urban environment settings. Researchers found that through activation of pumps, valves, and dosing mechanisms completely based on sensor data and thus automated, the scenarios of human error and labor requirements were minimized to the extent that the implementation of such systems by household and urban users with time or technical knowledge constraints is considered feasible. Real- time remote monitoring via web or mobile platforms significantly increases the system's usability and helps users to make the right decisions when managing their crops, thus sustaining the production of crops despite the limitations of the urban environment. Unfortunately, many articles only report experiments in highly controlled environments or short term trials and do not provide detailed long term performance and economic feasibility results. These shortcomings point to the necessity of further studies that are solar, powered and affordable, not only efficient, and can be scaled up IoT, based irrigation and nutrient management systems for urban farming in the local context of the Philippines (Reyes et al., 2022; Lim et al., 2023).

**IoT-Based Monitoring**

The IoT-based monitoring systems have had a tremendous impact on contemporary agricultural practice as they have allowed to monitor the environmental and plant growth parameters in real-time which are vital in precision farming. Literature sources highlight the point that IoT systems can be used to measure soil moisture, temperature, humidity, light intensity, and nutrient concentrations, followed by wireless communication and cloud computing to facilitate remote decision-making and automation (Zouari and Hachicha, 2025). These systems help to better manage the growth of crops as they will make it easier to notice the stress on the plants and optimize the irrigation and the application of nutrients, which will eventually lead to higher crop yields and resource efficiency. 

Moreover, the research under investigation of the controlled agricultural setting and greenhouse, aeroponic systems can prove that the IoT-based monitoring allows regulating the microclimatic conditions with high accuracy due to the real-time visualization of the data and the use of automated control systems, leading to the maintenance of stable growth conditions and the minimization of human interventions (Méndez-Guzmán et al., 2022). Further studies indicate that IoT-based plant surveillance systems can be used to achieve sustainability through the reduction of water and energy use and preservation of the optimal growing environment, especially under indoor and greenhouse-based growing conditions (DOAJ, 2021). Taken together, the literature reviewed confirms that IoT-based vegetation monitoring is quite advantageous regarding efficiency, accuracy, and scalability but the issues of implementing the system at reasonable costs, data analysis, and connectivity reliability at farm sites remain the focus of the research.

**Nutrient Regulation**

Local​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ studies on nutrient regulation in agriculture in the Philippines have shown that matching the nutrient supply to the specific needs of the crops is the key to improving the efficiency of the resources used and also to minimizing the pollution of the environment. Ocampo et al.'s early work on nutrient management made it clear that using site-specific nutrient schedules for maize—nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium regimens, in particular that are well calibrated—can increase yields by as much as 21% on average in comparison with conventional farmer practices, thus providing production under good growing conditions as strong evidence that nutrient availability remains the biggest production constraint. Nevertheless, these nutrient recommendations are still carried out manually, and they are not yet merged with automated irrigation or real-time sensing, thus exposing a large discrepancy between the theoretical optimization level and the implementation at the field level which is capable of regulating nutrients in different crop rows dynamically.

Mastul et al. also pointed out that the progressively indispensable role of IoT in agriculture in the Philippines could not be overemphasized as they cited the advantages of real-time monitoring, automated environmental control, and data-driven resource management to be very obvious for greenhouses, irrigation, and fields. However, on account of these benefits, the authors still acknowledged the problems that persist—for instance, the lack of good internet connection in remote areas and the high cost of IoT setup—which prevent the digital nutrient regulation platforms from being embraced by small-scale farmers. This suggests that nutrient management technology should be affordable, and it should be possible for it to operate even when there is intermittent or limited connectivity and, most importantly, in locations that are dependent on solar energy and microcontroller-based systems for reliable field operations.

Several local studies on solar-powered irrigation systems have brought in more ideas on the implementation of farm infrastructure powered by renewable energy. Guno and Agaton proved that solar irrigation cuts the farm's operational costs significantly, lowers the emission of pollutants, and, at the same time, stabilizes crop yields, while Mocando, Del Rosario, and Calagui documented notable improvements in water use efficiency and the corresponding reduction of the rural community's transport of fuel. However, although these systems have many benefits, it should be mentioned that they are, by their nature, designed to focus only on irrigation and thus, on the one hand, have neither the facility for the nutrient monitoring integration nor that for the control of fertigation, and on the other hand, have not solved the issue of different nutrient requirement in multi-row planting arrangements. Their research findings are consistent with the argument for developing water and nutrient delivery systems using solar power that do not simply treat these as two separate entities but rather as components that are interrelated.

The need for precision nutrient regulation can also be derived from the research of Cajucom et al. who discovered that the nitrate and phosphate levels of five vegetable-growing barangays in Nueva Vizcaya that exceeded the environmental limits set. According to their survey, nutrient mismanagement is rampant, and the local ecological system is at risk due to the overloading of phosphorus which leads to eutrophication of the water bodies in the downstream areas. It​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ is a fact that the environmental situation requires the control of nutrient application in a more precise way, but the researchers still haven’t brought real-time nutrient sensors, IoT monitoring, or automated corrective mechanisms into the picture—they simply show that there is a big gap in nutrient regulation technologies that are locally developed. 

According to local pieces of writing, considered a single body of work, the main points were: first, the agronomic importance of applying nutrients with great precision; second, the steadily increasing feasibility of IoT-based monitoring; and third, the sustainability potential of solar-powered farm systems. ​‍​

Yet, the existing pieces of research are still uncoordinated in that they are offering nutrient optimization without automation, reporting IoT benefits without fertigation application, showing solar-powered irrigation without nutrient control, and pointing at nutrient contamination without technological solutions. None of the local research works combine real-time nutrient sensing, automated multi-row fertigation, environmental feedback, and solar-powered autonomy into one operational platform. This gap is what necessitates the existence of a comprehensive nutrient-regulation system that not only can provide precise, but also automated and energy-independent nutrient and irrigation management which is specifically designed for the complex crop diversity of Philippine smallholder ​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌farms.

## **RELATED STUDIES (Foreign and Local)**

**Urban Vertical Farming**

Research on urban farming reveals that the use of technology, driven systems is a major factor in enhancing the productivity and sustainability of small- scale food production in urban settings. Several empirical studies have revealed that IoT enabled monitoring platforms with soil moisture, environmental, and nutrient sensors facilitate not only accurate real time tracking but also automated adjustments that can react to plant needs even more effectively than manual methods. It has been demonstrated that these systems can lessen water and nutrient wastes and at the same time increase plant growth results in household garden setups, thus proving their suitability for space, constrained urban environments and growers with limited time (Reyes et al., 2022; Ahmad et al., 2021).

Moreover, research centering on Arduino based control units and cheap sensor networks demonstrate that urban farmers can get their hands on affordable automated systems without having to deal with complicated infrastructure. Besides, the application of IoT connectivity allows remote monitoring and operation. Hence, growers can handle watering and feeding through internet or mobile interfaces, which not only makes it easier for them but also helps them make better decisions. Nonetheless, the literature also points out some drawbacks of the current applications, such as the lack of long term data on the performance and economic analyses to support the feasibility and the adoption of different urban contexts. These deficiencies emphasize the importance of further investigating strong, resource, efficient systems that are in real farming condition, ready, especially ones that use solar energy to achieve sustainability and energy independence (Lim et al., 2023; Patel & Shah, 2024).

**IoT-Based Monitoring**

Water ‍ ‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ resource management in a manner that is efficient alongside the optimization of agricultural productivity is among the primary goals of IoT-based irrigation systems. The recent publications highlight that the application of IoT devices in conjunction with sensors, microcontrollers, and wireless communication units leads to the on-demand checking and automatic irrigation that thereby cause the decreasing of manual labor and water waste by a great margin. This is because the systems that use soil moisture and environmental sensing combined with mobile or cloud platforms can switch on the pump automatically when the soil moisture level is lower than the set threshold and at the same time allow for the remote operation of a smartphone or dashboard, thus contributing both to the sustainability as well as to the operational efficiency of agriculture (Nawaz, Sial, & Raza, 2023). In addition, the intelligent modules that are equipped with neural network-based decision-making and are also low-cost can not only flexibly change their water schedules but also can be easily transported from one farmland to another. Still, at present, they have not yet been developed to the point where they are capable of managing multiple crops and regulating nutrients (Nawandar & Satpute, 2019).

The involvement of less polluting energy sources and more sophisticated automation algorithms have raised the bar for the performance of these setups even further. Indeed,​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ a solar-powered and fuzzy logic controlled system can do more than just improve water and energy efficiency. Such a system can also be a flexible and multi-mode operation system, e.g., local, mobile, and cloud control. Moreover, it helps in monitoring such parameters as soil moisture, temperature, humidity, and groundwater levels so that the pump can be protected from, e.g., overheating, etc. (Al‑Ali et al., 2020). The use of wireless sensor network has been a major factor in water conservation that is made possible by the implementation of low-cost architectures such as LoRa and ESP32 modules with full sensor data that can achieve up to a 34% reduction in water use. Consequently, this is indicative of the IoT potential to be a source of water-saving agriculture practices in different regions (Glória et al., ​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌2020). Also, such systems equipped with IoT-enabled microcontrollers and sensor networks can be shown to initiate irrigation upon reaching deep levels of precision and resource-efficiency in water management. Even so, these systems are frequently confined to limitations in multi-crop row management, nutrient regulation, and solar-powered operation, issues that the study intends to resolve. The research combining solar energy, multi-crop zoning, Arduino-based sensors, and nutrient monitoring aims at not only fully automated IoT irrigation but also energy-saving, precision-oriented, and thereby capable of fostering crop growth while limiting input waste (Rajeswari, Vignesh, & Sridharan, ​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌2020).

**Rainwater Harvesting**

Rainwater harvesting (RWH) systems are widely recognized as effective supplemental water sources for irrigation and agricultural sustainability, particularly under increasing climate variability and water scarcity. Existing literature emphasizes that RWH can significantly reduce dependence on conventional water supplies by capturing, storing, and redistributing rainfall for productive use, especially in irrigation and landscape applications. Studies highlight that RWH systems are most effective when integrated with demand-based water management strategies, as rainfall availability is highly seasonal and unpredictable due to climate change. Climate-driven variability affects storage reliability, runoff yield, and system sizing, necessitating adaptive designs that consider long-term rainfall trends and extreme weather events. These findings collectively demonstrate that RWH systems must be intelligently managed rather than operated as passive storage systems to ensure consistent irrigation performance (Campisano et al., 2017; Monjardin, 2023).

Advancements in irrigation control have further enhanced the efficiency of rainwater harvesting by combining it with soil moisture–based and depletion-based irrigation algorithms. Research shows that irrigation scheduling conditional on soil water depletion and daily precipitation improves water-use efficiency by delivering water only when crops require it, thereby minimizing water loss and over-irrigation. Such approaches are particularly effective for landscape and crop irrigation, where real-time soil and weather data can dynamically adjust irrigation timing and volume. These results support the integration of sensors and automated decision-making mechanisms in RWH-powered irrigation systems to optimize plant growth while conserving harvested rainwater (Cauteruccio & Lanza, 2022; Campisano et al., 2017).

Despite the demonstrated benefits, several limitations of rainwater harvesting systems have been identified, including limited storage capacity, high initial installation costs, maintenance challenges, and inconsistent rainfall distribution, particularly in upland and agricultural communities. Localized studies indicate that socio-economic constraints, lack of technical knowledge, and insufficient system automation can reduce the long-term effectiveness of RWH systems. These challenges underscore the need for automated, sensor-driven systems that can maximize water-use efficiency while reducing manual intervention. In relation to the present study titled “Automated Solar-Powered urban vertical farming IoT-Based Irrigation and Nutrient Regulation System for Efficient Vegetation Using Arduino-Based Sensors,” the reviewed literature provides strong justification for integrating rainwater harvesting with Arduino-based sensors, automated irrigation control, and renewable energy. Such integration directly addresses the identified limitations by enabling adaptive irrigation decisions, efficient water utilization, and sustainable operation in resource-constrained agricultural environments (Concepcion et al., 2013; Monjardin, 2023).

**Soil Moisture Sensor**

Recent foreign studies consistently emphasize the critical role of Arduino-based soil moisture sensors and sensor networks in improving agricultural water management and crop productivity. Across the literature, soil moisture monitoring systems are primarily designed to provide real-time, continuous data on soil water availability, which serves as a basis for informed irrigation decision-making. These systems commonly employ capacitive or resistive soil moisture sensors integrated with microcontrollers such as Arduino, enabling automated data acquisition and processing. The studies collectively report that sensor-based monitoring significantly enhances irrigation efficiency by preventing both overwatering and underwatering, which are common issues in traditional manual irrigation practices. This integrated approach is directly relevant to the present study, as the proposed automated solar-powered multi-crop irrigation system similarly relies on Arduino-based sensors to optimize water use across different crop rows and varying soil conditions (Shafira et al., 2022; Maghirang et al., 2024; Capalit et al., 2025; Melo et al., 2024).

In terms of technologies and system performance, the reviewed studies highlight the integration of soil moisture sensors with wireless sensor networks, IoT platforms, and automated control mechanisms. Data transmission is often facilitated through communication modules that allow remote monitoring and real-time visualization of soil conditions. Results from these studies indicate that such systems achieve high reliability in detecting soil moisture variations and demonstrate improved responsiveness in irrigation control compared to conventional methods. However, performance is influenced by factors such as sensor calibration, soil type variability, and environmental conditions. These findings are relevant to the proposed research, as the integration of Arduino-based sensors with IoT and automation in a solar-powered system must account for sensor accuracy and communication reliability to ensure effective irrigation and nutrient regulation for multiple crops (Shafira et al., 2022; Maghirang et al., 2024; Melo et al., 2024).

Despite their effectiveness, the literature also acknowledges several limitations that inform the design of the current study. Common challenges include sensor degradation over time, susceptibility to environmental interference, and limited scalability in larger or multi-crop farming setups. Some studies also note the lack of integration between soil moisture data and nutrient management, focusing primarily on irrigation control alone. These gaps directly justify the relevance of the present research, which aims to address such limitations by developing an automated, solar-powered, urban vertical farming system that not only regulates irrigation but also incorporates nutrient management using Arduino-based sensors. By building upon the strengths and addressing the shortcomings identified in prior studies, the proposed system seeks to contribute to more sustainable and efficient agricultural practices (Capalit et al., 2025; Melo et al., 2024; Maghirang et al., 2024).

**Nutrient Regulation**  
	Local​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ literature indicates that Philippine agriculture is gradually changing to fully automated sensor-based systems that both irrigation and nutrient regulation systems powered by renewable energy and informed by real-time environmental sensing. Ecija et al. through their pioneering research showed that soil-moisture-activated irrigation combined with water-level sensing is not only capable of maintaining optimal substrate conditions but also of reducing water and labor requirements, thus providing an indispensable reference point for precision irrigation systems in small farms. This certainly makes automated irrigation trustworthy; however, the scarcity of nutrient sensors or multi-crop adaptability in the systems highlights that a considerable proportion of these devices are still left unexposed to diversified field conditions.

Moreover, the local move towards the automation of agriculture focusing on nutrients is well exemplified by the work of Opamin et al. where hydroponics-based Arduino with a solar-tracking system helped in maintaining nutrient-rich oxygenated solutions, regulating temperature, and accelerating plant growth. The system which was a breakthrough in nutrient delivery, however, was only applicable to hydroponics and limited to single-crop setups, thus indicating that nutrient regulation technologies in the Philippines are still at the nascent stage of different system types and not yet evolved into the integration of soil-based multi-crop layouts.

Eusebio​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ et al. convincingly show the application of a solar-powered system as a means to more advanced nutrient regulation tools. Their study of AMTEC-tested photovoltaic irrigation pumps revealed that the energy consumption was reduced by about 45%, the cost saving was 30%, and the operational reliability was more than 98%. These accomplishments are the proofs that solar-powered systems are very reliable and have great potential for extensibility. However, the study only focused on hydraulic performance with a little emphasis on integrated nutrient-water management.

The research group of Valdez, Santos, and Cruz has partially closed this gap by presenting a creative automated fertigation system that used soil-matric-potential sensing and algorithmic control to detect and regulate moisture and nutrient levels in the agronomic standards. Their research outcomes showed not only the continuous vegetative growth but also the reduction of resource wastage, thus nutrient automation is viable in the Philippine environment, however, regrettably, it is still limited to single-zone fertigation without any extension to multi-row ​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌arrangements.

Additionally, the significance of nutrient monitoring is further supported by the case of a solar-powered aquaponics system designed by Garcia and Tibo, which incorporated IoT sensors to continuously monitor pH, nutrient concentration, temperature, and growth parameters. Their system became instrumental in improving resource efficiency and production output through constant nutrient-environment feedback loops. However, performance-focused on aquaponics limits the applicability of the concept to soil-based urban vertical farming thus emphasizing that there is still a lack of nutrient regulation devices specifically designed for the traditional multi-crop Philippine farms. 

Across all local literature, one conspicuous research gap is the absence of a unified nutrient and irrigation regulation system powered by solar energy, IoT-enabled, capable of handling multiple soil-based crop rows with differing nutrient requirements for that matter. The existing pieces of work have individually succeeded in automated irrigation, hydroponic nutrient delivery, or fertigation, but none of the previous Philippine research has brought about the integration of nutrient sensors, row-specific dosing, multi-zone irrigation, and solar autonomy into one single low-cost model suitable for smallholder environments. The lack of this integration points to the necessity for a comprehensive nutrient regulation system that can not only support diversified planting arrangements at the same time but also conserve resources and maintain the ability to be responsive in real-time to field conditions \- features that are still not available in the current local technological ​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌solutions.

## **Synthesis and Justification**

Internet of Things (IoT) and automation are the most popular technologies in the research as the important sources of efficiency in agriculture, especially in irrigation, environmental monitoring, and resource management. Research projects on solar ready irrigation systems such as solar powered irrigation systems illustrate the ability of clean energy to provide continuous agricultural production even in absence of traditional sources of power without depending on electricity power stations, curbing power shortages in remote or limited regions as well as lowering production costs. Other studies focus on sensor-based systems, such as soil moisture, temperature, and the nutrient sensors, to maximize the application of water and fertilizer. These sensors will respond to real-time field conditions and can be used to guarantee that irrigation and nutrients are delivered only where and when necessary and will enhance the overall crop performance.

The implementation of platforms that will be integrated with IoT is also noted, due to the convenience and efficiency provided by the application of IoT devices, where the irrigation schedule, the timely identification of environmental changes, and the timely interventions can be applied without the necessity of direct control on the site. The technologies based on microcontrollers, especially Arduino, have been advocated in enacting low-cost, extensible, and tailorable agricultural systems that could be applicable to the small scale in urban or domestic contexts. When taken together, automation, renewable energy, and sensor integration was proven to advance the current farming practices by enhancing efficiency, sustainability, and resource utilization.

Nevertheless, the majority of the existing solutions are oriented towards single-crop systems, irrigation systems, or detached nutrient systems without integrating these systems into a fully automated system. Moreover, not many studies use solar energy as the main source of energy that can be utilized in multifunction IoT because there is no adaptive nutrient control depending on particular needs of crops. These restrictions demonstrate the necessity of the system, which would integrate the solar-powered functioning, IoT-based real-time tracking, automated irrigation, and nutrient control in one system. The suggested IoT-based solar-powered irrigation and nutrient regulation system fills these gaps by offering a complex, environmentally-friendly and user-friendly solution, which incorporates Arduino-based sensors to deliver water and nutrients to the crops in an optimal way in the household or urban farming setting. This study targets to improve vegetation growth, resource conservation, and productivity in small-scale urban agriculture by discussing the flaws of previous studies, which have great prospects of accelerating sustainable agricultural methods.

**CHAPTER 3**

# **Automated Solar-Powered IoT-Based Irrigation and Nutrient Regulation System for Urban Farming Using Arduino-Based Sensors**

### 

### **Abstract**

Irrigation efficiency and proper nutrient management remain among the major challenges in modern agriculture, particularly in urban farming environments where space is limited and access to conventional power sources may be unreliable. Manual irrigation and nutrient application often result in inconsistent plant care, inefficient resource use, and suboptimal crop growth. An automated and intelligent farming system can reduce human intervention while improving crop growth efficiency and resource management. This study aims to develop an automated solar-powered IoT-based irrigation and nutrient regulation system for urban farming using Arduino-based sensors. The proposed system integrates soil moisture, nutrient-related, and environmental sensors into a single Arduino-based control unit to enable real-time monitoring and automatic regulation of irrigation and nutrient delivery. Solar energy is utilized to ensure continuous and sustainable system operation, while an Internet of Things (IoT) platform provides real-time data transmission and remote system monitoring. The use of Arduino-based sensor technology offers a cost-effective and practical approach to implementing automated irrigation and nutrient regulation. Overall, the developed system is intended to enhance water and nutrient use efficiency and promote sustainable urban farming practices.

**Keywords:** Urban Vertical Farming, IoT-Based Monitoring, Rainwater Harvesting, Soil Moisture Sensor, Nutrient Regulation

###  **Introduction**

Agriculture​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ continues to be a very vital sector of the economy in the Philippines, and it employs almost one-fifth of the total workforce of the country. Moreover, it is the main source of income for rural areas and also provides food security. Nevertheless, only a few smallholder and family farmers have the means to practice modern farming; thus, they still depend on traditional irrigation and fertilization methods that require a lot of manual labor and are susceptible to climate changes. As the extreme weather events become more frequent in different parts of the Philippines, such traditional farming methods are becoming more and more vulnerable, which eventually leads to the decrease of both productivity and resilience (World Bank, 2019). In such a situation, the use of renewable energy—mainly solar power—with sensors and Internet of Things (IoT) devices to modernize farming is a very viable solution. The country, the Philippines, is very compatible with such a change: among other things, it has a very strong solar potential, which is estimated to be around 4.5–5.5 kWh/m² per day, and the government’s support towards smart and green energy solutions is increasing (Department of Energy \[DOE\], 2020). The installation of solar-powered IoT devices in multi-crop row household gardens can facilitate the efficient use of water and nutrients, labor can be saved, and productivity can be increased in resource-constrained environments (World Bank, 2019; DOE, 2020).

Innovations in IoT, automation, and the use of renewables in agriculture have attracted a lot of research interest in a short time. The previous researches have shown that IoT application in precision farming leads to improvement of the environmental sustainability, increased yields, and better resource efficiency (Shamshiri et al., 2018; Navarro et al., 2020). For instance, the sensors built into the soil can gauge the moisture, temperature, pH, or electrical conductivity, and the internet-connected devices can start the pumps or valves, thus enabling real-time irrigation control. Navarro and his colleagues (2020), after thoroughly reviewing the farm IoT implementations, came to a conclusion that such systems provide opportunities for the ongoing soil moisture and environmental parameters monitoring, which in turn results in saving water and increasing crop output. The narrative reviews in the Philippines reveal that there is a growing trend in the use of IoT devices for crop monitoring, irrigation automation, and intelligent farming; however, there are still problems in terms of connectivity, cost, and technical capacity of rural farmers (Mastul et al., 2023). In the area of small-scale vegetable production, the national research agencies are putting the planting calendar and crop rotation as the two most important factors that contribute to the yield maximization in household gardens (Philippine Council for Agriculture, Aquatic and Natural Resources Research and Development \[PCAARRD\], n.d.).

Still, under the current systems, there are significant challenges that prevent the realization of the full potential of these promising developments. Most​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ of the agricultural IoT and solar-powered projects, which are transparently shown, are primarily intended for monoculture environments or large-scale commercial farms. Hardly any of them take into account the changing seasonal needs, crop rotation, or nutrient changes in mixed vegetable ​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌plots. Department of Agriculture says that in the Philippines, technology access in the countryside is mainly limited by the high cost and poor infrastructure, thus smallholders have a low rate of technology adoption due to these constraints (Department of Agriculture \[DA\], 2021). Also, even though a good number of irrigation systems are automated, nutrient regulation that is integrated, for example, automatic dosing of fertilizers based on soil moisture, electrical conductivity, and pH, is at a very early stage of development.According​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ to a recent study, the majority of Internet of Things (IoT) configurations are intended for greenhouses or single-crop systems, and the authors do not take into account the fluctuations of multi-crop, rotational farming in smallholder farms (Mastul et., 2023; Shamshiri et., ​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌2018). These deficiencies pinpoint the necessity for a flexible, affordable, and robust system that caters to smallholders in the Philippines.

This paper presents a proposal for an automated, solar-powered IoT-based irrigation and nutrient regulation system for urban household farming using Arduino-based sensors. The system integrates soil moisture and nutrient-related sensors with a solar energy supply to enable real-time monitoring and automated control of irrigation and nutrient delivery. When sensor readings indicate insufficient moisture or nutrient levels, pumps, valves, and dosing modules are automatically activated to supply the required water and liquid nutrients. In addition, the IoT-enabled architecture allows remote monitoring and control through a web-based or mobile interface. System performance will be evaluated based on irrigation efficiency, nutrient regulation accuracy, water-use efficiency, energy sustainability, plant growth response, and reduction in manual labor during household garden trials conducted in both dry and wet seasons. The study aims to demonstrate an affordable, data-driven, and environmentally sustainable model suitable for small-scale urban farming applications in the Philippines. 

The proposed research offers a sustainable, economical, and easily adoptable solution for urban households and community-based growers in the Philippines. The exclusive use of photovoltaic energy significantly reduces electricity costs and carbon emissions while ensuring continuous system operation. By optimizing water and nutrient delivery, the system minimizes resource waste and ensures that plants receive appropriate care according to their growth requirements. Automation further reduces the need for manual labor, making the system suitable for users with limited time or agricultural experience. Through continuous data collection and automated adjustments, the system promotes data-driven urban farming practices that can improve plant health and productivity. The technology supports national agricultural modernization initiatives, enhances household food security, and contributes to the achievement of the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals by encouraging sustainable urban and peri-urban farming goals by facilitating small-scale, community, urban, and peri-urban farming initiatives.

This research focuses on the design, development, and performance evaluation of a solar-powered IoT-based irrigation and nutrient regulation system for small-scale urban farming environments. The system emphasizes automated soil moisture monitoring, nutrient control, and operation powered solely by renewable energy. The study is limited to controlled household or small-garden settings and does not evaluate system performance in large-scale commercial agricultural operations. Factors such as long-term maintenance requirements, extreme weather conditions, and integration with advanced agricultural technologies are beyond the scope of this research. Additionally, the study does not include comprehensive economic analyses or long-term yield comparisons across different geographic regions.

**METHODOLOGY**

**Phase 1\. System Research and Data Collection**

Phase 1 is mainly concerned with the detailed study of the initial investigation and data-gathering processes that will lead to the development of the automated irrigation and nutrient regulation system for urban vertical farming. This phase of the project is a significant stage in understanding the system requirements and defining the parameters that will influence the overall system design. For this purpose, it is necessary to identify the factors of soil moisture and nutrient concentration that have the most impact on crop growth, to test the performance of selected sensors under small-scale conditions, and to assess the applicability of Internet of Things (IoT)-based farming technologies for urban vertical farming systems.

The fundamental aim of this stage is to furnish proper primary information which will later on be instrumental in the choosing of sensors, their calibration, and the determination of the system functionality in such a way that the automation system will be scientifically in line with the requirements of efficient and sustainable crop production.

***Step​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ 1: Identification of Key Parameters for Soil Moisture and Nutrient Concentration Recognizing*** 

       The most influential environmental and soil factors that directly affect plant growth and resource consumption in urban vertical farming systems are identified in this step. These include soil moisture levels, nutrient concentration requirements, and environmental variables such as temperature and humidity. These parameters serve as the groundwork for sensor selection and calibration in the automated system. The primary goal of this step is to determine the monitoring units and operational ranges that are essential for accurate sensing and automated decision-making.

***Step 2: Testing Sensor Behavior Under Small-Scale Farming Conditions***

In this step, selected sensors—including soil moisture sensors, nutrient analysis sensors (NPK or EC/TDS), and temperature and humidity sensors—are tested under controlled conditions to simulate small-scale urban vertical farming environments. Sensor responses are evaluated for consistency, sensitivity, and reliability to ensure accurate data collection. The testing process verifies that the sensors can effectively support automated irrigation, nutrient regulation, and environmental monitoring within the proposed system.

***Step 3: Review of IoT-Based Farming Technologies for Urban Vertical Farming Systems***

This step involves a comprehensive review of existing IoT-based farming technologies relevant to urban vertical farming and automated irrigation systems. The review focuses on communication modules, data acquisition platforms, power management techniques, and control strategies used in smart farming applications. Special attention is given to systems that support remote monitoring, automation, and integration with renewable energy and rainwater harvesting systems. The findings from this review guide the selection of appropriate technologies and system architecture for the proposed study.

**Phase​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ 2\. Development of the Automated Irrigation System**

       Phase 2 is the time when the entire automated irrigation system is fabricated, the working prototype is changed from the planning stage of the project to the functional stage. In this phase, hardware components are assembled, sensors are wired, and control algorithms are created. Also, the automation logic programming, which makes the system able to work without human intervention based on real-time sensor feedback, is part of it. Essentially, Phase 2 ensures that the system is a smart and responsive irrigation system where all the technical elements are integrated.

***Step 4: Integration of Sensors and Irrigation Hardware***

      In this operation, the system architecture is planned by linking all the required hardware components. This includes the integration of soil moisture sensors, nutrient sensors (such as NPK or EC/TDS), temperature and humidity sensors with an Arduino microcontroller, ESP8266 communication module, and LoRa SX1278 (if applicable). Pumps, valves, drip emitters, and irrigation lines are also connected to establish a fully functional irrigation structure suitable for **urban vertical farming setups**. The objective of this step is to construct a single physical system where all parts can communicate and operate together efficiently.

***Step 5: Development of Sensor Data Processing Algorithms***

       This stage focuses on developing the program code that enables the system to read, interpret, and analyze raw data obtained from the sensors. The programming logic includes handling data from soil moisture sensors, nutrient sensors, and temperature and humidity sensors, and converting these readings into meaningful values such as soil moisture levels, nutrient concentration values, and environmental parameters. Data accuracy and reliability are ensured through proper calibration and filtering techniques. The processed data serve as the decision basis for the automated irrigation and nutrient regulation system.

***Step 6: Programming of Automated Control Logic***

       At this point, the programming of the microcontroller is carried out using the processed sensor data to establish automated irrigation and nutrient regulation logic. Control logic is implemented to automatically signal the pump to turn on, open the valve, or activate nutrient delivery once predefined threshold levels are reached. The control logic represents a closed-loop system that continuously receives feedback from sensors and responds accordingly. This eliminates the need for manual intervention and allows the system to operate fully autonomously. The ultimate goal is to create a smart, self-regulated irrigation system capable of supplying appropriate water and nutrients for urban vertical farming.

**Phase 3\. Automation and Control Implementation**

The third phase focuses on the development and implementation of the automation and control mechanisms required to operate the irrigation and nutrient regulation system. This phase ensures that the pumps, valves, and control units function automatically based on sensor feedback. It also emphasizes reliability and consistency in system response under varying soil moisture and nutrient levels. By the end of this phase, the system is expected to operate autonomously, ensuring efficient irrigation management in an urban vertical farming environment.

***Step 7: Construct Relay-Controlled Water Pumps and Nutrient Solenoid Valves***

In this step, the researcher designs and connects relay-controlled water pumps and nutrient solenoid valves. These components are wired through the relay module, which is controlled by the Arduino microcontroller. Proper wiring of the pump, valve, solar panel, charge controller, and battery ensures that the system can operate reliably using solar power. The ON and OFF operations of the pumps and valves are controlled automatically based on the signals generated by the Arduino during testing and actual operation.

***Step 8: Develop Threshold-Based Automation Using Arduino Code***

In this stage, threshold-based automation logic is implemented using Arduino programming. Inputs from soil moisture sensors, nutrient sensors, and DHT22 sensors are processed, and threshold values are established to trigger appropriate actions. When soil moisture or nutrient levels fall below defined thresholds, the relay module activates the water pump or nutrient delivery system automatically. Communication modules transmit system data for monitoring and safety purposes, allowing the system to operate continuously without human intervention while enhancing irrigation efficiency and accuracy.

| Sensor | Reading/ Threshold | What it Means | System Action |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Soil Moisture | 30%–45% | Normal water level for plants | Maintain regular irrigation |
|  | 20%–29% | Slightly dry soil | Increase watering slightly; send notification |
|  | \<20% | Soil too dry | Max irrigation; alert user |
| Temperature | 20–35 °C | Ideal plant growth temperature | Maintain current irrigation schedule |
|  | \<20 °C | Cold conditions | Reduce irrigation; alert user |
|  | \>35 °C | Heat stress risk | Increase irrigation slightly; monitor plants |
| Humidity | 50%–85% | Normal ambient humidity | Continue system operation |
|  | \<50% | Air is dry | Adjust irrigation if necessary; notify user |
|  | \>85% | High humidity | Monitor for disease risk; reduce irrigation if needed |
| Nutrient Concentration | 800–1,200 ppm | Optimal nutrient level | Maintain fertilizer dosing |
|  | 600–799 ppm | Slightly low | Increase nutrient dosing automatically |
|  | \>1,200 ppm | Slightly high | Reduce nutrient dosing automatically |
|  | \<600 ppm or \>1,400 ppm | Critical nutrient imbalance | Stop system; correct nutrient concentration immediately |
| Irrigation Frequency | Normal (as scheduled) | Watering is on schedule | Maintain current schedule |
|  | Too frequent | Overwatering risk | Reduce irrigation |

**Table 1\. Sensor Baseline Thresholds and System Actions**

***Step 9: Integrate solar-powered system (solar panel, battery, charge controller) to supply energy to the system.*** 

This step involves integrating the solar-powered energy system composed of a solar panel, charge controller, and battery to supply power to the automated irrigation and nutrient regulation system. The solar panel is installed to capture solar energy, which is regulated by the charge controller and stored in the battery for continuous system operation. This setup ensures a stable and renewable power supply for the Arduino microcontroller, sensors, pumps, communication modules, and control circuitry. The integration of the solar-powered system allows the urban vertical farming setup to operate independently of conventional power sources and supports sustainable, energy-efficient system performance.

**Phase 4\. Prototype Construction** 

Phase 4 extends the automation subsystem from a laboratory setup into a complete physical prototype suitable for urban vertical farming. The prototype integrates control units, pumps, water tanks, nutrient tanks, rainwater harvesting components, and irrigation lines into a unified structure. The outcome of this phase is a fully assembled prototype capable of supporting automated irrigation, nutrient regulation, and rainwater utilization.

***Step 10: Build the prototype structure for multiple crop rows using lightweight, weather-resistant materials.***

The physical frame that supports the planting medium and irrigation components is constructed using lightweight and weather-resistant materials such as PVC pipes and metal frames. The structure is designed to support vertical planting arrangements, allowing efficient use of limited urban space. Structural stability is tested by gradually loading planting beds with soil and water and checking for bending or sagging.

**Step 11: Install Sensors, Pumps, Tubing Systems, Water Tank, and Nutrient Tank**

Following the construction of the main structure, sensors and major subsystems are installed and interconnected. The water tank is placed at a safe and reachable location, slightly elevated to support efficient pumping. The irrigation pump is connected to the water tank, while tubing distributes water across vertical planting rows. A nutrient tank is installed with a dosing pump or solenoid valve to regulate nutrient delivery. A **rainwater harvesting system** is integrated to collect and store rainwater, which supplements the water supply and promotes sustainable irrigation.

Soil moisture sensors are placed strategically within planting beds, EC/TDS or NPK sensors are positioned near nutrient distribution points, and temperature and humidity sensors are installed near the canopy to monitor microclimate conditions. All tubes and cables are securely fastened to prevent displacement or damage.

***Step 12\.  Integrate the control circuitry and ensure proper wiring between modules.***

The electronic components such as the Arduino microcontroller, relay modules, power supply units, and the IoT communication module (if available) were fixed on a control panel or enclosed housing and wired to the sensors, pumps, solenoid valves, and rainwater collection components installed in the previous step. All electrical and signal connections were carefully checked against the system schematic to ensure correctness and to prevent the possibility of improper wiring. A preliminary dry-run test was conducted wherein the pumps were briefly activated to draw water from the main water tank and, when required, a test solution from the nutrient tank and rainwater storage container.

This operation ensured that fluid passages, valve directions, rainwater flow paths, and electrical connections were functioning as expected. Voltage readings were taken at critical points in the circuit to confirm that power delivery from the solar-powered system was stable and safe throughout the urban vertical farming setup. Any loose wiring, leakage, or logical inconsistencies identified during testing were corrected. After completing these adjustments, the prototype was considered fully functional and ready for the succeeding phase of IoT-based monitoring and experimentation.

**Phase​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ 5\. IoT Integration and Monitoring System Development**

In Phase 5, Internet of Things (IoT) technology is implemented to provide remote monitoring and data logging capabilities for the urban vertical farming system. At this stage, the inputs consist of the fully assembled prototype, including the irrigation system, nutrient delivery system, rainwater harvesting components, sensors, water and nutrient tanks, microcontroller, and IoT communication module, together with the selected cloud-based platform.

The outputs of this phase include a functional IoT dashboard capable of receiving and displaying real-time data such as soil moisture levels, nutrient concentration, irrigation activity, rainwater utilization status, and system operational conditions. The dashboard presents information in a user-friendly format, allowing remote supervision, record-keeping, and performance monitoring of the automated irrigation and nutrient regulation system.

***Step 13\. Connect Arduino to an IoT Platform***

An ESP8266 or a similar Wi-Fi communication module was configured to enable the Arduino microcontroller to interact with a designated IoT service such as Blynk or ThingSpeak. Network credentials were written into the system code, and test messages were transmitted to verify that the microcontroller was able to upload sensor readings and operational states reliably. This includes monitoring scenarios where irrigation pumps draw water from the main water tank or nutrient solution from the nutrient tank, as well as tracking system operation within the urban vertical farming setup. Successful data transmission confirmed that the system was capable of supporting real-time remote monitoring.

***Step 14\. Program real-time data transmission for soil moisture, and nutrient levels.***

In this step, the firmware was enhanced to support both local control operations and the continuous recording of sensor readings and system status. The collected data were organized and transmitted to the cloud server at scheduled intervals. Parameters sent included soil moisture levels, irrigation activity, nutrient dosing events, and system operational status relevant to the urban vertical farming system. To address possible network interruptions, error-handling routines were implemented to initiate automatic reconnection attempts during Wi-Fi dropouts, ensuring uninterrupted data transmission and continuous system monitoring.

***Step 15\. Develop a user interface to visualize system readings and control status remotely.***

An IoT-based monitoring dashboard was developed to display data collected from the **urban vertical farming system** in a systematic and real-time manner. Soil moisture and electrical conductivity (EC) readings were presented through charts, while system indicators showed the operational status and recent activation logs of the irrigation pump connected to the water tank, the nutrient dosing unit linked to the nutrient tank, and relevant system alerts. This interface enabled remote supervision, operational diagnostics, and monitoring of irrigation and nutrient regulation activities, including those supported by harvested rainwater.

| Trigger Condition | Data Sent to IoT Platform | Notification Shown to User | Purpose of Notification |
| :---- | :---- | ----- | :---- |
| Soil moisture falls below 30% | Soil moisture value | “Soil moisture is low. Irrigation activated.” | Inform user that watering has started |
| Soil moisture reaches optimal range (30%–45%) | Updated soil moisture value | “Soil moisture is back to normal. Irrigation stopped.” | Confirm successful irrigation |
| Nutrient level below 800 ppm | Nutrient concentration (ppm) | “Nutrient level is low. Nutrient dosing activated.” | Notify nutrient correction |
| Nutrient level exceeds 1,200 ppm | Nutrient concentration (ppm) | “High nutrient level detected. Dosing stopped.” | Prevent over-fertilization |
| Irrigation pump turns ON | Pump status | “Water pump is running.” | Confirm irrigation activity |
| Irrigation pump turns OFF | Pump status | “Water pump stopped.” | Confirm end of irrigation |
| Nutrient pump turns ON | Pump status | “Nutrient dosing in progress.” | Inform dosing activity |
| Nutrient pump turns OFF | Pump status | “Nutrient dosing completed.” | Confirm nutrient delivery |
| Water tank level low | Water level status | “Water tank level is low. Refill required.” | Prevent system interruption |
| Wi-Fi connection lost | Connection status | “Connection lost. Attempting to reconnect.” | Inform user of network issue |
| Wi-Fi reconnected | Connection status | “System reconnected successfully.” | Confirm system stability |

**Table 2: Proposed IoT Notifications for Remote Monitoring and Control**

Users can remotely check through this interface if environmental parameters are kept within the specified agronomic thresholds and also they can monitor the number of irrigation and nutrient supply events by a mobile device or a computer. By bringing together sensor outputs and system operations into one analytical platform, the dashboard is a core instrument for performance evaluation, operational diagnostics, and ongoing system refinement of automated irrigation and nutrient ​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌regulation.

**Phase 6\. System Evaluation and Performance Assessment**

This phase focuses on carrying out an in-depth assessment and making system improvements to the automated irrigation and nutrient regulation system after its deployment in real urban vertical farming scenarios. At this stage, the prototype is evaluated in terms of responsiveness, accuracy, and operational stability under different environmental conditions. The objective is to determine whether the system works reliably when subjected to real-life situations such as weather changes, soil moisture variation, and crop water demand. Performance measurement, detection of hardware or software issues, and adjustment of operational parameters are conducted to ensure the system becomes more precise, stable, and capable of supporting efficient irrigation and nutrient management in urban vertical farming systems.

### ***Step 16: Conduct Field Testing to Monitor System Response Under Varying Weather and Soil Conditions***

This step involves deploying the system in actual urban farming environments to conduct hands-on testing beyond laboratory conditions. Various weather patterns and soil moisture conditions are applied to test the automated irrigation and nutrient management processes, allowing evaluation of how the system responds to different scenarios. This step also includes verifying that sensors properly respond to changes in temperature, humidity, and soil moisture, and that the control logic correctly issues irrigation and nutrient delivery commands. The goal of this stage is to ensure that the system behaves accurately and in a timely manner under real agricultural conditions relevant to urban vertical farming.

### ***Step 17: Evaluate Sensor Accuracy, Water Efficiency, and Plant Growth Performance***

In this stage, the overall efficiency of the automated farm management system is evaluated through the assessment of sensor accuracy, quantification of water consumption, and observation of plant growth performance. The collected data provide insight into whether the sensors are reliable in detecting soil moisture and environmental changes, whether the water-saving approach implemented through automated irrigation is effective, and whether the plants grow uniformly and vigorously under controlled conditions. The core aim of this step is to confirm that the system contributes to efficient and sustainable urban vertical farming by delivering water and nutrients in a precise and timely manner.

***Step 18: Troubleshoot Hardware and Software Inconsistencies***

In this step, issues identified during field testing are addressed and analyzed. These problems may include incorrect sensor readings, delays in data communication, hardware malfunctions, or errors in the control logic. Troubleshooting activities involve sensor recalibration, repair or replacement of faulty components, firmware updates, and code modification to eliminate system errors. This step ensures that both hardware and software components operate harmoniously, thereby improving system reliability and maintaining the trustworthiness of the automated irrigation and nutrient regulation system.

### ***Step 19: Refine System Thresholds and Calibration for Improved Reliability***

The final step focuses on optimizing the system based on the results obtained from field testing and troubleshooting. This process involves adjusting soil moisture thresholds, timing intervals, calibration settings, and response triggers to enhance overall system reliability. These refinements ensure that the system delivers water and nutrients with greater precision and consistency. Through optimization of thresholds and calibration parameters, the automated irrigation and nutrient regulation system becomes more flexible, reliable, and efficient for prolonged operation in urban vertical farming applications.

**Theoretical Framework**

The​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ theoretical basis of this research is basically derived from the combined insights of RRL and RRS, which, among other things, call for the significant role of automation, sensor technologies, IoT connectivity, and the use of renewable energy in agriculture of the future. According to the studies included in the review, soil moisture sensors, environmental monitoring, and Arduino-based microcontrollers not only accurize irrigation but also make it possible for irrigation to be water-responsive since the water management is based on the latest field data. The argument of the research cited in the solar-powered agricultural systems section is that renewable energy will be a solution to the continuous and cost-efficient operation problem. Especially in remote areas where the power supply is unreliable. The research on IoT-enabled farming also shows that by using wireless communication and remote monitoring, farmers can control irrigation and nutrient delivery in a more efficient way and at the same time, the use of labor and resources can be minimized. The​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ concepts outlined here are the theory that forms the basis of automated, data-driven decision-making in precision agriculture.

The research articles that have been examined frequently signal the same kind of gaps that are being cited by the different pieces of literature in the field. That is mostly systems concentrating solely on irrigation, single-crop setups, or isolated monitoring tasks, and very few that combine solar power, IoT automation, sensor-based irrigation, and nutrient regulation into one unit. This gap is what underpins this research, which is committed to the merging of these concepts to form a single solution. Consequently, the theoretical framework identifies the Automated Solar-Powered Urban Vertical Farming IoT-Based Irrigation and Nutrient Regulation System as a very first evidence of the existing theories extension and application wherein sensors are used for accurate monitoring, Arduino for automated control, IoT for communication, and solar energy for eco-friendly ​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌operation.

**Figure 1: Conceptual Framework**

The study follows a systems-based conceptual framework illustrating the development and operation of an Automated Solar-Powered IoT-Based Irrigation and Nutrient Regulation System. It begins with baseline data collection and system design, followed by automation and prototype development. Sensor data serve as inputs for control algorithms that manage water and nutrient delivery, while IoT technology enables real-time monitoring. The final phase involves field testing, evaluation, and optimization to enhance system performance in small-scale urban or household farming settings.

### **Statement of the Problem**

This research aims to develop a smart urban vertical farming system that leverages IoT technology and Arduino-based sensors to automatically manage irrigation and nutrient delivery, ensuring efficient plant growth in small-scale household or urban gardens.

The study, in particular, will articulate research objectives as follows:

1. **Design** an automated system running on solar-powered that utilizes sensor data for controlling soil moisture and nutrient regulation in crops.

2. **Develop** water and nutrient regulation system that performs the distribution of these resources without human intervention and is guided by the sensor data obtained in ​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌real-time. 

3. **Construct** a functional prototype integrating Arduino-based sensors, control units, and a Rainwater Harvesting for urban vertical farming.

4. **Implement** a network of things (IoT)-based monitoring system that delivers in real-time data and status of the system. 

5. **Detect** variations in the soil and atmospheric conditions and take action to keep the best levels for the development of the ​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌crops. 

**Keywords:** Urban Vertical Farming, IoT-Based Monitoring, Rainwater Harvesting, Moisture Sensor, Nutrient Regulation

**Significance of the Study**  
The outcome of this study will be useful to the small-scale farmers and household gardeners because it will offer them an automated irrigation and nutrient control system that could guarantee an optimum growth of the plants and minimize the number of individuals required to work in the farms. By supporting efficient water and nutrient utilization, which are made possible by real time monitoring and automated interventions, the system makes the crops healthier and more productive. Additionally, the research is a good source of reference and learning material to agricultural institutions, researchers and students. It illustrates how the IoT and solar-powered technologies can be used in practice as effective solutions to smart and sustainable urban farming. The system is also a foundation of future studies on precision agriculture, automation, and friendly farming practices to the environment. Besides this, renewable energy coupled with automated irrigation and nutrient management can promote efficiency of resources and environmental sustainability. This research is therefore useful towards achieving the responsible and sustainable goals of agricultural development.

**Scope and Limitations**   
The current paper provides the design and prototype of an Automated Solar-Powered IoT-Based Irrigation and Nutrient Regulation System that is to be applied in household gardens and urban farming, on small-scale-level. The system employs Arduino sensors to measure the amount of soil moisture, temperature, humidity, and nutrients in the soil, and automatically regulates the amount of irrigation and nutrient delivery within set limits. The IoT platform allows users to monitor the plant and keep records in real-time to effectively control its growth.  
The research is however restricted to small scale implementation, short-term testing and simple functional assessment. The main indicator of sensor accuracy is the good calibration of the sensor and the environment, whereas the constant connection to the internet is necessary in real-time monitoring. There are no sophisticated applications to the system like weather prediction, detection of pests, and prediction of crops yield.

**Definition of Terms**  
The operationally-defined terms to be employed in this study are the following:  
Arduino \- is an open-source microcontroller platform that will be used to read sensor data as well as control irrigation and nutrient dosing components of the system.  
Soil Moisture Sensor- it is a tool used to gauge the content of water in the soil to know when it requires irrigation.  
Temperature Sensor- sensor is put in place in order to measure the ambient temperature conditions, which influence the growth of a plant.  
Humidity Sensor- a gadget that is utilized in the determination of moisture content of the air surrounding the plants.  
Nutrient Sensor (EC/TDS Sensor)- a sensor that checks the level of nutrient concentration in the irrigation solution in parts per million (ppm).  
Irrigation System- the mechanicalized system that feeds crops with water according to sensors.  
Nutrient Regulation System \- this is a computerized manipulation of nutrient supply to plants based on a predetermined level.  
Internet of Things (IoT) \- the system, which enables sensors and devices to deliver data to its web-based platform to be remotely monitored and operated.  
IoT Dashboard \- a web or mobile interface that is utilized to show real time sensor values and system status.  
Solar Power System- a source of renewable energy made up of solar panels and other associated materials which convert them into electrical power to power the system.  
Automated System- the system which is operated with less human intervention, that is, it is programmed with logic control.

**Research Design**

The study will follow an experimental research design that is aimed at testing the performance of the Automated Solar-Powered IoT-Based Irrigation and Nutrient Regulation System in a domestic garden. The system will react to the existing gardening conditions, involving automatic irrigation and message of nutrients to the soil through the sensor information obtained in real-time during the experiment. The design will help to record the growth of selected crops and the level of moisture in the soil, water used, the condition of the environment, and the responsiveness of the system that will make it possible to estimate the overall performance of the system. By means of controlled testing and observation under the normal residential gardening conditions, the research will establish the accuracy, efficiency, and overall functionality of the system in the automation of water and nutrient control in urban or household farming.

**Research Setting**

The study will be carried out in a controlled household vertical farming setup where the small scale urban or residential gardening will be emulated. The research location will consist of soil beds, water supply lines, nutrient reservoirs, solar panels and Arduino based sensors to measure soil moisture, temperature and humidity. It will simulate the routine tasks that a household gardener performs which includes; irrigation, nutrient application and monitoring of the plant species giving an authentic setting of growth of crops. This garden will be a suitable location to test the functionality of the Automated Solar-Powered IoT-Based Irrigation and Nutrient Regulation System. It will enable accurate tracking and control of water consumption, nutrient supply, the environment, and the general performance of the automated system in controlling urban farming in the house.

**Respondents of the Study**

The​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ research will revolve around the selected elements of the urban vertical farming setting that were considered as the main participants. The choice of these will depend on their role in the optimal crop growth and efficient resource management. By focusing on such specific components as Arduino-based soil moisture sensors, temperature and humidity sensors, irrigation pumps, nutrient delivery modules, and solar-powered units, the research is designed to determine the effectiveness of the automated system in regulating water supply, nutrient distribution, and environmental conditions for multiple crops. The​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ data obtained from these instruments will be a major factor in figuring out the possible raising of irrigation precision, nutrient uniformity, and general gardening productivity by means of a locally implemented IoT-based, solar-powered automated ​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌system.

The research, while centering on system-level optimization, also recognizes the impact of household gardener behavior on plant care and therefore intends to find out in what way the automated system can effectively accompany and facilitate the usual gardening ​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌practices.

**Data Gathering Procedures**

The collection of data in this study will be initiated by preparing and calibration of Automated Solar-Powered IoT-Based Irrigation and Nutrient Regulation System to be installed in an urban vertical farming setting. First, baseline measurements will be taken to allow baseline conditions to be established concerning the vertical farm. They are soil or growth medium moisture (30%-45% range of threshold), temperature (20 o C \-35 o C), humidity (50 percent-85 percent), irrigation rate and the nutrient levels (800-1,200 ppm). These baseline values are required to be recorded in order to have a comparison of normal growing conditions prior to the automated operation of the system.

The environment and growth medium conditions will also be continuously observed once the farms are in operation using Arduino-based sensors in-built in the vertical farming system. The nutrient delivery and irrigation modules will be automatic and will only react to the pre-programmed thresholds. The sensor measurements and system logs as they are retrieved via the IoT monitoring dashboard will give real-time data about water consumption, nutrient application, and system responsiveness without the need to manually source this information. Besides automated monitoring, there will be daily manual checks of the plant growth, health and system performance to ensure that automated operations are running as expected. The process of data collection will be continued during the experiment. The comparison of the post-implementation measurements with the baseline values will then be used to measure the performance of the system according to the irrigation efficiency, nutrient management, and crop development on the whole in a vertical urban farming environment.

**Data Gathering Instruments**

The​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ study will harness technology such as electronic sensors, automated units, and manual tools for data collection necessary for the system’s performance evaluation. Among the major devices is a soil moisture sensor built on Arduino that detects the soil’s water content. Also, a temperature–humidity sensor helps in recording the environmental variables that affect plant growth. Further, the system’s automated irrigation and nutrient delivery units can be the data sources by giving out the logs of the water distribution patterns, the nutrient application intervals, and the activation responses. A solar power monitoring unit will keep tabs on the energy captured and used by the solar-powered parts during the test. The readings from all digital devices and the system’s activities will be brought together via an IoT monitoring interface that keeps the real-time sensor outputs and the operational logs. Additionally, manual tools like growth meters and observation sheets will help in recording the physical plant measurements and the visible crop health indicators. These devices will be instrumental in accurate and complete data gathering for the ​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌research.

**Statistical​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ Treatment of Data**

The sensor data gathered will go through statistical analysis to evaluate the performance and the energy-saving of the Automated Solar-Powered Urban Vertical Farming IoT-Based Irrigation System.

Descriptive statistics like mean and standard deviation will be used to represent the data of soil moisture, water usage, pump activations, and environmental conditions collected. These words will help to clarify how the system can maintain the soil moisture level, regulate the water consumption, and respond to the environmental changes.

The paired t-test will be utilized for comparing the average daily soil moisture and water usage per row before and after the system implementation. This will confirm whether the automated system brings statistically significant improvements in irrigation efficiency. The analyses will be carried out with the proper statistical software, and the significance level will ​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌be p\<0.05.

1. Mean 

Where:

* Xˉ \= mean  
* ∑ X \= sum of all observations  
* n \= total number of observations

2\. Standard Deviation

Where:

* SD \= standard deviation  
* X \= each observation  
* Xˉ \= mean  
* n \= total number of observations

3\. Paired t-test

![][image1]

Where:

* t \= t-value  
* dˉ \= mean difference between pre-implementation and post-implementation values  
* sd \= standard deviation of the differences  
* n \= number of paired observations
