**APPENDIX A**

| List of Equipments |  |  |  |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **Components** | **Image** | **Description** | **Application** |
| **Solar Panel** | **![][image2]** | The solar panel is a unit that changes solar energy into electrical energy. It delivers power to the battery and system during the day. | The system is kept running with clean energy from the sun which is a great way to save up on power from other sources. |
| **Solar Charge Controller** | ![][image3] | The solar charge controller is a device that manages the energy coming from the solar panel to the battery in a safe way and also, it prevents the battery from overcharging or over-discharging. | It is a device that offers three types of protection to the battery: overcharge and over discharge protection, and power management efficiency. |
| **Battery** | ![][image4] | The battery is the one that keeps the system running when there is no sunlight or at night. It is able to deliver the energy that it stored during the day from the solar panel.  | It is the one that supplies the power during the night or when it is cloudy and thus keeps the sensors and communication modules working.  |
| **Water Motor  Pump** | ![][image5] | A device powered by a microcontroller that takes in water and nutrient solution to the irrigation system and is activated when a sensor detects low soil moisture or nutrient levels. ​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ | The​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌ water motor pump is the one that carries water and nutrient solution from the storage tank to the irrigation lines as a part of the automated nutrient regulation system. |
| **Arduino UNO** | ![][image6] | Arduino UNO is a single-board microcontroller that can read sensor inputs, control output devices, and do other data processing operations necessary for automation.  | It is the main controller that processes sensor readings and commands actuators or communication modules. |
| **ESP 8266** | ![][image7] | The ESP 8266 is a low-priced WiFi module, which is used for wireless communication or can be used for sending data to a server or cloud (if WiFi is available).  | Make sensor data available online via a web server, database, or cloud platform (if WiFi-connected). |
| **NPK Sensor** | ![][image8] | The NPK Sensor is a soil sensor that identifies the concentration of Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus (P), and Potassium (K) in the soil — these three being the most essential nutrients for plant growth.  | The device can be used to assess the condition of the earth and, thus, correctly dispense fertilizer for a better plant growth.  |
| **DHT 22 Temperature Humidity Sensor** | ![][image9] | The DHT 22 is a digital sensor that measures air temperature and humidity with decent precision.  | It is used to monitor the environmental conditions that influence the health and growth of plants.  |
| **TDS Sensor / EC sensor** | **![][image10]** | A TDS/EC sensor measures the electrical conductivity of water or nutrient solution to estimate the total concentration of dissolved nutrients. | the sensor monitors nutrient levels and enables automated fertilizer dosing through the Arduino-based solar-powered IoT irrigation system. |
| **Moisture Sensor** | ![][image11] | The Moisture Sensor is a device that measures the water content of the soil and can indicate whether the soil is dry, moist, or wet.  | Automates watering activities by issuing a command to the pump or by sending a notification when the soil is too dry.  |
| **LCD** | ![][image12] | The LCD (Liquid Crystal Display) is a commonly used display for Arduino projects, as it provides a simple way to output information to the user such as text and basic characters. | LCDs  are used to display sensor data, project status, menus, and custom messages, making projects interactive and user-friendly, |
| **Water Sensor** | ![][image13] | The water level sensor is an electronic sensor that detects the presence and level of water in a container or tank. It provides real-time feedback to the microcontroller regarding water availability. | The water level sensor is used to monitor the water level in the rainwater storage tank and nutrient tank. This prevents the water pump from operating when water levels are low and helps ensure safe and efficient irrigation system operation. |
| **Servo Motor** | ![][image14] | The servo motor is a small rotary actuator that allows precise control of angular position. It is commonly used in Arduino-based systems to control mechanical movements accurately based on programmed commands. | The servo motor is used to control the opening and closing mechanism of the Rainwater Harvesting. Based on rain sensor input or system conditions, the servo motor automatically directs or blocks rainwater flow into the storage tank, enabling efficient and controlled rainwater harvesting. |
| **Rainwater Sensor** | ![][image15] | The rainwater sensor is a sensing device designed to detect the presence of rain by measuring surface moisture or conductivity caused by rainfall. | The rainwater sensor is used to detect rainfall events and signal the system to collect rainwater or temporarily suspend irrigation during rain. This helps optimize water usage and supports sustainable water management in the urban vertical farming system. |

**APPENDIX B**

**MATRIX OF REVIEW OF RELATED LITERATURE (Foreign and Local)**

| Author (Year) | Urban Vertical Farming | IoT-Based Monitoring | Rainwater Harvesting | Soil Moisture Sensor | Nutrient Regulation |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Rufí-Salís, M. (2020) | Focuses on urban agriculture systems designed for limited city spaces, applicable to controlled and vertical farming environments. |  |  |  | Highlights closed-loop nutrient recirculation, reducing nutrient loss and improving nutrient use efficiency in urban agriculture. |
| Pascual et al. (2018) | Implements vertical hydroponic farming for crop production in the Philippines. |  |  |  | Manages nutrient solutions for plant growth in hydroponics. |
| Campisano et al. (2017) | Applicable to dense urban settings through rooftop and building-integrated systems supporting urban agriculture | Not the primary focus; emphasizes system assessment and management rather than real-time IoT. | Reviews design, implementation, and performance of urban rainwater harvesting systems for water reuse and stormwater mitigation. |  |  |
| Capalit, et al. (2025) | Applicable to small-scale and urban farming plots. | Implements IoT-based soil moisture monitoring with low-cost hardware. |  | Focuses on soil moisture sensing for irrigation control. |  |
| Martin et al. (2025) | Explores vertical farming integrated into urban buildings; uses residual space efficiently for food production |  |  |  | Circular fertilizers from brewery and biogas waste mentioned for sustainability |
| DA-AFID (2022) | Highlights urban vertical farms in the Philippines (e.g., NXTLVL Farms), government initiatives, and policies promoting urban agriculture. |  “Digital agriculture” and automated systems conceptually for improving farm productivity. |  |  | Hydroponics and vertical farming techniques are mentioned, involving manual nutrient management in growing solutions. |
| Muthmainnah et al. (2024) |  | ESP8266 microcontroller and Blynk platform used to monitor soil moisture and temperature in real-time. |  | Uses YL-69 sensor (98.71% accuracy) for precise soil moisture measurement. |  |
| Hussen & Ahmed (2025) | Reviews vertical farming techniques in urban horticulture, including hydroponics and soilless systems; emphasizes sustainable urban food production. |  |  |  | Discusses nutrient delivery in hydroponic and vertical farming systems to optimize plant growth. |
| Ali, De, & Maity (2025) | Explains vertical farming as a method of growing crops in stacked layers within urban areas, emphasizing space maximization, local food production, and sustainability. |  |  |  | Discusses precise nutrient delivery through hydroponics, aeroponics, and aquaponics, reducing fertilizer usage and improving efficiency. |
| Gunapala et al. (2025) | Discusses urban agriculture and vertical farming as strategies for sustainable food production in cities, reducing reliance on pesticides and fertilizers, and enhancing local food security. |  | Water management techniques like rainwater harvesting, drip irrigation, bio-swales, and water reuse are highlighted to conserve freshwater in urban farms. |  |  |
| **Cundiman, L et al. (2026)** | Construct a functional model that combines urban vertical farming with sensors and automatic control units for the simultaneous application of irrigation and nutrients. | Implement a network of things (IoT)-based monitoring system capable of monitoring that delivers in real-time data and status of the system. | Entire setup runs using energy from solar panels and battery | Uses soil moisture, temperature, humidity, and NPK sensors to track plant and soil conditions. | Develop water and nutrient management system that performs the distribution of these resources without human intervention and is guided by the sensor data obtained in ​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌real-time.  |

**APPENDIX C**

**MATRIX OF REVIEW OF RELATED STUDIES (Foreign and Local)**

| Author (Year) | Urban Vertical Farming | IoT-Based Monitoring  | Rainwater Harvesting | Soil-Moisture Sensor | Nutrient Regulation |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Cauteruccio & Lanza (2022) | Relevant to urban landscape and urban farming irrigation planning. | Uses modeling and data-driven approaches that can be integrated with smart monitoring systems. | Develops and evaluates rainwater harvesting systems optimized for irrigation using soil water balance concepts. | Incorporates soil water depletion concepts linked to soil moisture conditions. |  |
|  Shafira et al. (2022) | Relevant to urban horticulture and smart farming applications.  | Implements IoT-enabled monitoring systems. |  | Uses soil moisture sensors for real-time irrigation decisions.  |  |
| Ng et al. (2023) | Focuses on vertical farming to maximize space in urban environments | Uses IoT to monitor soil moisture, temperature, and humidity |  | Monitors soil moisture for plant water needs |  |
| Dim & Guillermo, 2022 | Implemented Arduino-based vertical farming for basil, lettuce, and spinach | Real-time monitoring using Arduino sensors for soil moisture and environment | Uses soil moisture sensors to automate irrigation |  |  |
| Raju et al. (2019) |  | Proposes an IoT-based system using Raspberry Pi for real-time soil moisture monitoring and automated irrigation; connects to weather API for efficient water management. |  | Uses sensors to detect soil moisture and automatically control irrigation systems. |  |
| Wayangkau et al. (2021) |  | Developed an Arduino-based IoT system to automatically monitor soil moisture and temperature for precision agriculture. |  | Uses soil moisture sensors connected to Arduino to monitor and maintain optimal soil conditions. |  |
| Anitha et al. (2019) |  | Uses IoT to monitor soil moisture in real-time and allow remote access via mobile phones. |  | Monitors soil moisture continuously for optimal irrigation scheduling using IoT. |  |
| Sudarmaji et al. (2023) |  | Uses IoT ESP32 with capacitive soil moisture sensors to continuously monitor soil moisture remotely via web interface and Google Sheets. |  | Uses capacitive soil moisture sensors with ESP32 to track soil water content for irrigation control. |  |
| Islam et al. (2023) |  | Developed an IoT device with sensors for real-time monitoring of soil moisture, humidity, temperature, and nutrient (NPK) levels; data transmitted to cloud platform for analysis. |  | Uses FC-28 moisture sensor to measure soil water content continuously. | Monitors NPK nutrient levels in real-time and provides crop-specific recommendations using ML algorithms. |
| Rufí-Salís et al. (2020) | Conducted on rooftop greenhouses (urban agriculture systems), focusing on crop growth in urban environments using hydroponic closed-loop systems. |  | Closed-loop system recycles irrigation water, reducing water consumption by 40%. |  | Closed-loop system recirculates nutrients, saving 35–54% of NPK inputs; allows assessment of nutrient efficiency and deficiencies in crops. |
| **Cundiman, L. et al. (2026)** | Construct a functional model that combines urban vertical farming with sensors and automatic control units for the simultaneous application of irrigation and nutrients. | Implement a network of things (IoT)-based irrigation system capable of monitoring that delivers in real-time data and status of the system. | Entire setup runs using energy from solar panels and battery | Uses soil moisture, temperature, humidity, and NPK sensors to track plant and soil conditions. | Develop water and nutrient management system that performs the distribution of these resources without human intervention and is guided by the sensor data obtained in ​‍​‌‍​‍‌​‍​‌‍​‍‌real-time.  |

**APPENDIX D**

| MATRIX OF METHODOLOGY |  |  |  |  |  |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **Stages** | **Research Settings** | **Respondents Of The Study** |  **Data Gathering Procedures** | **Data Gathering Instruments** | **Statistical Analysis** |
| **Phase 1: System Research and Data Collection** | Controlled small-scale farm area; Indoor lab/workshop; Library and online research | Environmental variables and system components; Soil moisture levels; Nutrient concentrations; Temperature; Humidity; Sensor behavior | Identify soil moisture and nutrient needs; Test environmental parameters and sensors; Review IoT-based farming technologies | Soil moisture sensors; Nutrient sensors; Temp-humidity sensors; Arduino modules; Documentation logs; Academic databases |  |
| **Phase 2 Development of the Automated Irrigation System** | This will be done at home with laboratory equipment.  Designated for developing and coding for irrigation system | The researchers acted as evaluators who constructed the architecture, developed the algorithms, and implemented the control logic. | The system was integrated, calibrated, programmed, and tested to evaluate its performance. | Arduino IDE handled programming, sensors processed data, and valves and pumps acted as outputs.  | N/A |
| **Phase 3\. Automation and Control Implementation**  | Controlled laboratory and small test farm. | Researchers (hardware setup, programming, calibration). | Configure Arduino-LoRa system; test under varying soil conditions. | Solar and electronic components, sensors, pumps, valves, data recording tools. | Descriptive statistics, variance, percentage analysis of automation performance. |
| **Phase 4\. Prototype Construction \- Build structure; install sensors, pumps, water tank, nutrient tank, tubing; integrate wiring.** | Outdoor household garden Prototype testing area | System hardware components Structural frame Soil moisture sensors DHT22 sensor Irrigation pump Nutrient pump Solenoid valve Water tank Nutrient tank Tubing and wiring | Frame assembly Tank and pump installation Sensor mounting Tubing connection Wiring integration Water flow checking Nutrient flow checking | Prototype frame materials Sensors and pumps Water and nutrient tanks Irrigation tubing Control circuitry Wiring connections | None |
| **Phase 5\. IoT Integration and Monitoring — Development of a real-time monitoring dashboard and the capability of wireless data transmission.** | Wi-Fi enabled environment IoT hardware setup Cloud-based platform | IoT communication modules ESP8266 / ESP32 devices System data users | Microcontroller – IoT interfacing Internet packet transmission via wireless means. Sensor data acquisition System validation tests Remote monitoring accuracy | Blynk/ThingSpeak dashboard Mobile and web interface Arduino Serial Monitor Internet connection. | Descriptive Summaries (range, frequency, consistency of transmitted data) |
| **Phase 6: System Evaluation and Optimization** | Real farming site for field testing; lab/workshop for troubleshooting and recalibration. | Respondents include system outputs, sensor readings, water usage records, environmental changes, and plant growth performance metrics. | Conduct field testing under varying soil and weather conditions; evaluate sensor accuracy, water use, and plant growth; troubleshoot system issues; refine thresholds and calibration. | Soil and environmental sensors, data loggers, water usage meter, plant growth measurement tools, diagnostic tools. | T-test |
